7 min read
Just now

--

There’s a particular kind of frustration that comes from mentoring someone. You see the bug immediately. You could just say the answer out loud and save everyone twenty minutes. But you don’t, because you know that if you just hand it over, they’ll forget it by next week.
That tension — between explaining and letting someone struggle — sits at the heart of how software teams actually learn. And there are three techniques that resolve it, each built for a different moment: the Socratic approach, rubber duck debugging, and the Feynman technique. They’re not interchangeable. They’re not really competing with each other either. Each one shows up at a different point in the learning process, and together they cover most of what “learning to think like an engineer” actually involves.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Why knowledge transfer usually fails
In most dev teams, knowledge transfer collapses into one of two extremes. Either the mentor just tells the person the answer — fast, but nothing sticks — or the developer is left to struggle alone for hours — real learning happens, but it’s brutally inefficient.
These three techniques exist specifically to dissolve that trade-off. Each works through a different mechanism, in a different context, but they all point at the same outcome: turning surface-level knowledge into something that actually sticks.
The Socratic approach: don’t answer, ask
“Don’t give the answer — ask the right question.” That’s the whole idea, and it traces back to Socrates’ dialectical method: the belief that people reach the truth not by being told it, but by being guided toward it through pointed questions. Applied to mentoring, this means that instead of solving the problem for the developer, you help them find their own way to the solution.
The point isn’t to be cryptic or withhold help for its own sake. It’s that a developer who arrives at an answer through their own reasoning remembers it — where one that’s simply handed the fix often doesn’t. This matters most with junior engineers, whose problem-solving instincts are still forming.
This approach really earns its keep during mentoring sessions and code reviews — moments when a developer hits a bug or has to make a design decision. What it’s not good for: production incidents. If something’s on fire, this is the wrong time to teach through questions. Fix first, teach later.
Here’s a scenario that shows the technique in action. An intern kept hitting an out-of-bounds index error inside a loop. Instead of pointing at the line, the mentor asked:

• “What do you expect this code not to do?”

• “What’s actually happening here?”

• “Where do these two things diverge?”

• “On which iteration does the loop fail?”
Answering those questions, the intern spotted the broken boundary condition themselves. That kind of discovery sticks in a way a handed-over fix never does.
There’s a real way to get this wrong, though. The most common failure is letting “why” questions slide into an accusatory tone — “why did you write it like this?” puts someone on the defensive immediately. Questions that start with “what” tend to land better: “what was the reasoning behind this decision?” instead. Keeping questions too abstract is another trap — “how could you have done this better?” is vague; “what should this function actually be responsible for?” gives them something concrete to grab onto. And a good Socratic mentor isn’t uncomfortable with silence. Leaving room for someone to think is worth more than jumping in with an answer.
Rubber duck debugging: talk to something that can’t talk back
“Explain the code to a rubber duck, and the bug reveals itself.” This one comes from The Pragmatic Programmer (Hunt & Thomas, 1999), where it got its name and its popularity. The mechanic is almost embarrassingly simple: a developer explains broken code out loud, line by line — to a rubber duck, a coworker, or an empty room — and the gap between what the code is supposed to do and what it actually does tends to surface on its own.
Most people assume the power is in the duck. It isn’t. The real mechanism is something psychologists call the self-explanation effect — saying something out loud engages different parts of the brain and slows down the thought patterns that go on autopilot when you’re just staring at your own code.
This technique lives during debugging, specifically when code behaves unpredictably or a bug is reproducible but its source isn’t obvious.
Here’s a scenario worth walking through. A developer was debugging a double-payment bug in a banking app. The code already had an idempotency check, but the bug was still happening. So they started explaining it to a rubber duck sitting on their desk:

• “First, I receive the payment request…”

• “Then I check the idempotency key…”

• “If it hasn’t been processed before, I process the payment…”
And that’s exactly where they stopped talking. The retry mechanism was running before the idempotency check. Which meant that when the same request fired twice, the second attempt skipped the check entirely. The bug surfaced itself, purely from the act of narrating the code out loud.
The biggest mistake people make with this one is treating it as a cute habit instead of a serious debugging tool — and skipping it for anything that feels like a “real” problem. In practice, it holds up even against gnarly concurrency bugs. A few things worth knowing: the duck can be a colleague instead (this is basically pair debugging in disguise), speaking out loud beats reading silently every time, and writing the explanation down works too if you’re not in a place where you can talk to yourself.
The Feynman technique: explain it like the person doesn’t know anything
“If you can’t explain it simply, you don’t really understand it.” Nobel laureate physicist Richard Feynman was famous for making even the densest quantum physics concepts sound almost obvious. This technique borrows his approach directly: the most reliable way to test whether you actually understand something is to try explaining it as if you were talking to a five-year-old.
The process breaks down into four steps. First, pick a concept — something you think you’ve learned, whether that’s an algorithm, a system design, a design pattern, or a business process. Second, explain it in plain language, no jargon, no technical shortcuts, as if you were talking to someone with zero background in the topic. Third, notice where you get stuck — the parts you can’t explain, the parts you skip over, the parts where you catch yourself saying “and then, you know, sort of…” Those are your actual gaps in understanding. Fourth, go back to the source, fill those gaps, and try the simple explanation again. Repeat until the explanation holds up cleanly.
This one is for after you’ve learned a concept, when you want to check whether the learning was real or just felt real.
Here’s an example. A junior developer was prepping a presentation on a fraud detection model for stakeholders. Their first pass sounded like this:

• “We’re using gradient boosting with ensemble learning…”

• “Feature engineering involves temporal aggregation…”

• “We’re optimizing false positive rate through threshold tuning…”
Watching the stakeholders’ faces go blank, they stopped and tried again: “The system evaluates every transaction like a security guard. It watches your habits, and if it sees something unusual, it raises an alarm.” And in reworking the explanation, they noticed something else — they couldn’t actually articulate why the threshold had been set at 0.7. The Feynman technique had done double duty here: sharpening the explanation for the audience, and exposing a gap in the developer’s own understanding.
People tend to mix this up with rubber duck debugging, but they’re built for genuinely different situations. Rubber ducking happens in the moment of debugging, when code isn’t working. The Feynman technique happens in the moment of learning, when you’re checking whether you actually understand. One hunts for bugs; the other tests comprehension.
Another trap: stopping after one pass. Explaining something simply once isn’t the finish line. Real mastery means being able to go a layer deeper too — answering “okay, but why does it work that way?” just as plainly as the first explanation. And the technique isn’t limited to talking out loud, either. Writing, sketching, building a diagram — anything that moves you from passively consuming information to actively producing it works the same way.
Side by side
Socratic ApproachRubber Duck DebuggingFeynman TechniqueWhenMentoring, code reviewWhile debuggingAfter learning a conceptCore goalEncourage independent thinkingSurface the gap between intent and realityExpose gaps in understandingDirectionMentor → developerDeveloper → self (or a duck)Developer → self (or someone else)MechanismGuiding questionsSelf-explanation, out loudJargon-free simplificationOriginSocrates’ dialectical methodThe Pragmatic Programmer (1999)Richard Feynman’s teaching styleWorks in a crisis?NoYesNoMost common mistakeLetting “why” sound accusatoryNot taking it seriouslyConfusing it with rubber ducking
How they actually fit together
These three techniques show up at different points along a developer’s learning path, and they complement each other almost too neatly.
Use the Socratic approach when you’re teaching someone else, or being mentored yourself. When a developer brings you a question, resist handing over the answer. Ask the right question instead, and let them find their own way to the fix. It deepens the learning for both of you.
Reach for rubber duck debugging when the code simply won’t work. Instead of staring at the screen for another hour, explain it out loud — to a duck, a coworker, an empty room, it doesn’t matter. Talking it through shifts your brain into a different mode, and the bug tends to surface on its own.
Turn to the Feynman technique when you want to test whether you’ve actually learned something. Try explaining the new concept in plain language. Every spot where you slip into jargon is a marker — a real gap in your understanding, waiting to be closed. Keep repeating the cycle until the explanation holds.
None of these are alternatives to each other. Picking the right one for the right moment is what actually transforms how a team mentors, debugs, and learns.
